title: Four metres, forty metres
summary: After a small-town baseball game, a father who shouted from the stands wants to argue with the umpire. The umpire turns the argument around.

body.speaker: Mr Fischer
body.text: Coach! Wait. I want to talk to the umpire.

body.speaker: Mr Kowalski
body.text: I am here.

body.speaker: Mr Fischer
body.text: You called my son out. He was safe.

body.speaker: Mr Kowalski
body.text: I called what I saw. I am four metres away. You are forty metres away.

body.speaker: Mr Fischer
body.text: You are wrong.

body.speaker: Mr Kowalski
body.text: May I ask how old your son is?

body.speaker: Mr Fischer
body.text: Nine.

body.speaker: Mr Kowalski
body.text: And you want him to play when he is twelve, sixteen, twenty?

body.speaker: Mr Fischer
body.text: Yes.

body.speaker: Mr Kowalski
body.text: The game I called today is small. Your son will play one thousand games. Some umpires will be right. Some will be wrong. The boy who can survive a wrong call at nine becomes a man who can play at twenty. The boy who hears his father shout becomes a man who cannot.

body.speaker: Mr Fischer
body.text: That is not fair.

body.speaker: Mr Kowalski
body.text: It is fair. I did not say you were a bad father. I said you were a loud father. The two are not the same.

body.speaker: Mr Fischer
body.text: I was loud.

body.speaker: Mr Kowalski
body.text: Yes.

body.speaker: Mr Fischer
body.text: My son saw me shout.

body.speaker: Mr Kowalski
body.text: Your son heard you.

body.speaker: Mr Fischer
body.text: Should I say something to him?

body.speaker: Mr Kowalski
body.text: Yes. Not 'I was wrong.' Say 'Next time I will be quiet, and you will play your game.'

body.speaker: Mr Fischer
body.text: Thank you. May I shake your hand?

body.speaker: Mr Kowalski
body.text: Always.

annotations.meaningVi: (thuật ngữ bóng chày) bị loại tại căn cứ
(thuật ngữ bóng chày) an toàn / đến căn cứ kịp
Cho phép tôi hỏi — cách hỏi lịch sự bằng 'may I'
sống sót qua / vượt qua (khó khăn)
quyết định sai / lần phán quyết nhầm của trọng tài

annotations.phrase: The two are not the same
annotations.meaningVi: Hai cái không giống nhau — cách diễn đạt tương phản cố định
lần sau / lần tới — cụm trạng từ chỉ thời gian
im lặng / yên lặng (trái nghĩa với loud)
to tiếng / ồn ào (về âm thanh hoặc hành vi)

grammarNotes.title: Past simple of 'to be': was / were
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Was' đi với I/he/she/it; 'were' đi với you/we/they. Trong bài: 'He was safe.' 'You were a loud father.' 'I was loud.' 'I was wrong.' Phủ định: 'was not' (wasn't) / 'were not' (weren't). Câu hỏi: 'Was he safe?' / 'Were you loud?' Đây là dạng quá khứ của động từ 'to be' — không cần 'did' để hỏi hay phủ định.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Was' goes with I/he/she/it; 'were' with you/we/they. The dialogue: 'He was safe.' 'You were a loud father.' 'I was loud.' Negatives: 'was not' / 'were not'. Questions: 'Was he safe?' / 'Were you loud?' Unlike other past simple verbs, 'to be' does not use 'did' for questions or negatives.

translationVi:
Ông Fischer: Huấn luyện viên! Đợi. Tôi muốn nói chuyện với trọng tài.
Ông Kowalski: Tôi ở đây.
Ông Fischer: Ông cho con tôi bị loại ở căn cứ hai. Nó về kịp mà.
Ông Kowalski: Tôi gọi đúng cái tôi thấy. Tôi cách đó bốn mét. Ông cách đó bốn mươi mét.
Ông Fischer: Ông sai.
Ông Kowalski: Cho phép tôi hỏi: con ông mấy tuổi?
Ông Fischer: Chín.
Ông Kowalski: Chín. Và ông muốn nó chơi khi mười hai, mười sáu, hai mươi?
Ông Fischer: Đúng.
Ông Kowalski: Trận tôi gọi hôm nay nhỏ. Con ông sẽ chơi một nghìn trận. Có trọng tài sẽ đúng. Có người sẽ sai. Đứa bé sống sót qua một lần gọi sai năm chín tuổi trở thành một người đàn ông chơi được ở hai mươi. Đứa bé nghe cha mình hét trở thành một người đàn ông không chơi được.
Ông Fischer: Vậy không công bằng.
Ông Kowalski: Công bằng. Tôi không nói ông là một người cha tệ. Tôi nói ông là một người cha to tiếng. Hai cái không giống nhau.
Ông Fischer: Tôi đã to tiếng.
Ông Kowalski: Đúng.
Ông Fischer: Con tôi thấy tôi hét.
Ông Kowalski: Con ông nghe ông.
Ông Fischer: Tôi có nên nói gì với cháu không?
Ông Kowalski: Có. Không phải 'Bố đã sai.' Hãy nói 'Lần sau bố sẽ im, và con sẽ chơi trận của con.'
Ông Fischer: Cảm ơn. Tôi bắt tay ông được không?
Ông Kowalski: Lúc nào cũng được.

questions.prompt: What does Mr Fischer want to discuss?
options: A call the umpire made — that his son was out at second base
questions.explanation: 'You called my son out. He was safe.'

questions.prompt: How does Mr Kowalski answer the distance argument?
options: 'I am four metres away. You are forty metres away.'

questions.prompt: What does Mr Kowalski say about the game today?
options: It is small; the son will play one thousand more games.
It was the most important game ever.
questions.explanation: 'The game I called today is small. Your son will play one thousand games.'

questions.prompt: What does Mr Kowalski say happens to the boy who 'hears his father shout'?
options: He becomes a man who cannot play at twenty.
questions.explanation: 'The boy who hears his father shout becomes a man who cannot.'

questions.prompt: What does Mr Kowalski say he did NOT say?
options: He did NOT say Mr Fischer was a bad father; he said he was a loud father.
He did not say it was a game.
He did not say his son was nine.
He did not say anything at all.
questions.explanation: 'I did not say you were a bad father. I said you were a loud father.'

questions.prompt: How does Mr Fischer change in the dialogue?
options: He moves from blaming the umpire to admitting 'I was loud.'

questions.prompt: What does Mr Kowalski tell him to say to his son?
options: 'Next time I will be quiet, and you will play your game.'
questions.hint: Mr Kowalski gives the exact words.

options: Mr Fischer asks to shake the umpire's hand; the umpire says 'Always.'
Mr Fischer leaves without a word.
questions.hint: Look at the last two lines.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the dialogue?
options: A small wrong call is less damaging to a child's long sporting life than a loud parent — and a calm adult can sometimes redirect the argument away from the call.
questions.explanation: Mr Kowalski shifts the conversation from the call itself to the long-term effect of the father's shouting.
questions.hint: Notice what Mr Kowalski does NOT defend (the call), and what he focuses on instead.

blanks.explanation: He wants to talk to the umpire.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mr Kowalski tells Mr Fischer that the wrong call is small but the shouting is large. Is there a place in your own life — at home, at work, with a child — where you have been arguing about the wrong, small thing while the loud thing was the real issue? What would you say if you spoke about the loud thing instead?
